one_liner: A 66-year-old woman presents with 4 months of progressive exertional dyspnea, orthopnea, and fatigue; she denies chest pain or syncope.

stage2.hpi_extension: She now struggles to walk one block without stopping, sleeps on three pillows, and wakes at night gasping for air (paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea). She had a small inferior MI 3 years ago that was treated with a stent. She denies palpitations or fever. No rheumatic fever or IV drug use.
stage2.pmh: Coronary artery disease (s/p PCI 3 years ago), hypertension, hyperlipidemia
stage2.physical_exam: Comfortable at rest but dyspneic with walking. Cardiac: holosystolic (pansystolic) blowing murmur at the apex radiating to the left axilla. Soft S1. Laterally displaced apical impulse. S3 gallop present. Murmur increases with handgrip (increased afterload). Lungs: bibasilar crackles. Extremities: trace pitting edema bilaterally. JVD to 8 cm.

lab_results.result: Transthoracic echocardiogram: severe mitral regurgitation with an eccentric posteriorly-directed jet from posterior leaflet restriction (ischemic/tethered). Dilated left atrium (5.2 cm) and left ventricle. EF 40% with inferior wall hypokinesis. Pulmonary artery systolic pressure 48 mmHg.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Severe MR with LA and LV dilation and reduced EF indicates chronic regurgitant volume overload and ischemic MR from prior inferior MI with papillary muscle dysfunction.
BNP is released by ventricular myocytes in response to wall stretch from volume or pressure overload — elevated in heart failure from severe MR.

lab_results.result: Sinus rhythm with occasional PACs. Left atrial enlargement (P mitrale: broad, notched P waves in lead II). Q waves in inferior leads from prior MI.
lab_results.clinical_significance: P mitrale reflects chronic left atrial enlargement from regurgitant volume. Inferior Q waves confirm prior infarction as the ischemic substrate for papillary muscle dysfunction.

stage5.final_clue: She is started on afterload reduction with an ACE inhibitor and diuretic, and referred to cardiothoracic surgery for mitral valve repair before further left ventricular decompensation.

debrief.classic_presentation: Chronic MR presents insidiously with fatigue, exertional dyspnea, orthopnea, and eventual heart failure symptoms. The hallmark murmur is a holosystolic blowing murmur at the apex that radiates to the axilla, best heard in left lateral decubitus position. Acute MR (e.g., from papillary muscle rupture post-MI) presents dramatically with flash pulmonary edema and cardiogenic shock.
debrief.pathophysiology: MR causes a regurgitant volume jet from LV to LA during systole, reducing forward stroke volume and causing LA volume/pressure overload. Chronic MR leads to eccentric LV hypertrophy (sarcomeres added in series) and LV/LA dilation to accommodate the regurgitant volume — eventually progressing to systolic dysfunction and pulmonary hypertension. Common etiologies include mitral valve prolapse (most common in developed countries), ischemic papillary muscle dysfunction or rupture (post-MI), rheumatic disease, infective endocarditis, and LV dilation (functional MR).
debrief.treatment_overview: Chronic MR: afterload reduction with ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or vasodilators decreases regurgitant fraction. Beta-blockers and diuretics for heart failure symptoms. Definitive therapy is mitral valve repair (preferred) or replacement — indicated for symptomatic severe MR or asymptomatic patients with LVEF 30–60% or LV end-systolic diameter ≥40 mm. Acute severe MR (papillary muscle rupture) requires emergent surgery.
high_yield_pearls: Holosystolic blowing murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla = mitral regurgitation. Loudest at the apex in left lateral decubitus.
Murmur increases with handgrip (↑afterload → ↑regurgitant fraction) — useful to distinguish MR from HCM (which decreases with handgrip).
Mitral valve prolapse is the most common cause of MR in developed countries; classically presents with a mid-systolic click that may be followed by a late systolic murmur.
Papillary muscle rupture typically occurs 3–5 days post-MI; the posteromedial papillary muscle is most vulnerable because it has a single blood supply (PDA).
Chronic volume overload causes eccentric LVH (sarcomeres in series) → dilated LV, contrasted with the concentric LVH (sarcomeres in parallel) of pressure overload.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Cardiovascular chapter, 'Valvular heart disease — Mitral regurgitation' section.
